title: Polymyalgia Rheumatica
oneLiner: An inflammatory condition of the elderly causing bilateral proximal shoulder/hip stiffness and pain, with dramatically elevated inflammatory markers, exquisitely steroid-sensitive – and a paschal lamb for giant cell arteritis.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:** A 72-year-old woman presents to the ED with “I just can’t get out of bed in the mornings.” She describes bilateral shoulder and hip stiffness that lasts over an hour, making it impossible to comb her hair or rise from a chair. She has no weakness (can lift arms once the stiffness wears off), no joint swelling, and no fever. Yesterday she felt exhausted and ached all over. Bloods show ESR 95 mm/hr, CRP 140 mg/L, and a borderline normocytic anaemia. Creatine kinase (CK) is normal. She is tearful, frustrated, and wonders if it’s “just old age.”

**Dissecting the clues:**

- **Age > 50 years (especially >65)** – defines the population; PMR rarely occurs before 50.
- **Bilateral proximal pain and stiffness** – shoulders and hips are typical; “proximal” is the hallmark – distinguishes from osteoarthritis (distal DIP joints) or rheumatoid (MCP/PIP).
- **Morning stiffness > 45–60 minutes** – inflammatory pattern; distinguishes from mechanical pain (stiffness < 10 min) and fibromyalgia (non-specific, all-day ache).
- **No true weakness** – intact power on formal testing; if weakness is present, think polymyositis or cervical radiculopathy.
- **Dramatic elevation of ESR/CRP** – acute phase response is almost always present; normal ESR is a red flag (see atypical).
- **Normal creatine kinase (CK)** – rules out primary muscle disease.
- **Constitutional symptoms** – fatigue, low-grade fever, weight loss – can be prominent and mislead toward malignancy.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

1. **“Normal” ESR variant** – Up to 20% of PMR patients have ESR <40 mm/hr if also low CRP; but CRP is almost always elevated. Clue: typical clinical picture with elevated CRP and normal ESR – still PMR.
2. **Isolated monoarticular or asymmetric onset** – e.g., only one shoulder or only hip pain. Often mislabeled as trochanteric bursitis or rotator cuff tear. Clue: absence of mechanical cause, elevated CRP, age >60.
3. **Concurrent GCA with PMR** – Patient may have typical PMR symptoms but also reports new-onset headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, or visual disturbances. Don’t miss the “halo sign” on temporal artery ultrasound. This changes management (high-dose steroids).
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:** Polymyalgia rheumatica is an inflammatory condition that attacks the lining of the large proximal joints (shoulders, hips) and the bursae (fluid-filled sacs) around them, causing intense morning stiffness and pain. It is not a myopathy – muscles are structurally normal – but the inflammation in the joint lining makes movement painful. The body mounts a massive inflammatory response, which is why blood markers like ESR and CRP go sky-high.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** One of the most common inflammatory rheumatologic conditions in the elderly; incidence rises to ~1 per 1000 in people over 60.
- **Stakes:** It is exquisitely treatable with low-dose steroids, yet often undiagnosed for months because “it’s just old age.” Delay leads to prolonged disability, and – more critically – PMR is the harbinger of **giant cell arteritis (GCA)** in 15–20% of patients. GCA can cause irreversible blindness, stroke, and aortic dissection.
- **Common pitfalls:** Prescribing NSAIDs alone (inadequate), starting steroids without checking inflammatory markers first (lost diagnostic opportunity if response is used as a criterion), and forgetting to ask about GCA symptoms.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story:** PMR is an autoimmune/autoinflammatory disease of unknown trigger. It likely begins with an antigenic insult (possibly infectious) that activates CD4+ T helper cells and macrophages in genetically susceptible individuals (HLA-DRB1*04). These cells infiltrate the synovium of proximal joints (glenohumeral, hip) and the extra-articular bursae (subacromial, trochanteric, iliopsoas). The resulting inflammation is dominated by interleukin-6 (IL-6), which drives the acute-phase response.

**Why each key sign happens (mechanism → sign):**

| Pathophysiology | Clinical Sign |
|----------------|--------------|
| Synovitis and bursitis of glenohumeral and hip joints – inflammation thickens synovium, causing pain when moved. | Bilateral shoulder and hip pain; stiffness after rest because inflammatory fluid accumulates during sleep. |
| IL-6 from activated macrophages stimulates liver to produce large amounts of CRP and fibrinogen. | Markedly elevated CRP and ESR; fibrinogen contributes to hyperviscosity (rare cause of headache or blurred vision). |
| IL-6 also suppresses erythropoietin and increases hepcidin → impaired iron utilisation. | Normochromic, normocytic anaemia of chronic disease; mild reactive thrombocytosis (platelets often >450). |
| Inflammation is **not** in muscle fibres – no myositis, no elevation of muscle enzymes. | Normal CK; preserved power on exam (patient can lift arm but hurts to do so). |
| Systemic IL-6 effects on hypothalamus: pyrogen action, reset thermoregulatory centre. | Low-grade fever, fatigue, malaise, weight loss – constitutional symptoms that mimic infection or malignancy. |
| Same inflammatory process can involve extracranial branches of the carotid artery if GCA develops. | Jaw claudication, headache, scalp tenderness, visual disturbances – these are **not** PMR per se, but a shared vasculitic process. |

**Connect the pattern recognition cues:** The morning stiffness is the direct symptom of **inflammatory fluid accumulation** overnight – it improves as the fluid is moved. The absence of weakness confirms the pathology is around the joint, not in the muscle. The high ESR/CRP is the IL-6-driven signature – both are used to monitor activity.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Common Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** (most common) | No identifiable trigger; likely autoimmune + genetic predisposition. | Elderly patient, insidious onset, no preceding illness. |
| **Infectious trigger** (seasonal/viral) | Human metapneumovirus, RSV, parvovirus B19; often preceded by upper respiratory infection (URI). | “Had a cold 3 weeks ago – now everything hurts.” |
| **Genetic** | HLA-DRB1*04, particularly subtypes *0401 and *0404 in Northern Europeans. | No specific clue, but consider if family history of PMR or GCA. |
| **Giant cell arteritis (associated)** | 15–20% of PMR patients have concurrent or future GCA. | Patient with PMR develops headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, sudden vision loss. |
| **Drug-induced?** | Rare; interferon-α reported to trigger PMR. | On treatment for hepatitis C or cancer with interferon → new stiffness. |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic. Environmental triggers (e.g., winter/spring peaks, recent URI) are accepted but not proven. Always ask about recent infections – but in practice, most cases have no clear precipitant.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria (clinical, not scoring):** The commonest used are the **2012 ACR/EULAR classification criteria** (primarily for research) but for daily practice we use **Bird’s criteria** (1979):
- Age > 50 years
- Bilateral aching and morning stiffness > 30 minutes
- ESR > 40 mm/hr (or CRP > 50 mg/L)
- Exclusion of other inflammatory conditions (RA, polymyositis, infection)
- **Key: rapid response to prednisolone ≤20 mg/day** (often used as a therapeutic test).

**Specific tests:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | Interpretation |
|------|--------------|-------------|---------------|
| **ESR & CRP** | Markedly elevated (ESR often >80, CRP >50). | Confirm inflammatory state; essential for diagnosis and monitoring. | Both elevated – typical PMR. Normal CRP is very unusual. If ESR low but CRP high – still PMR. If both normal – reconsider diagnosis. |
| **Full blood count** | Normocytic anaemia, thrombocytosis, mild neutrophilia. | Part of workup for chronic inflammation; rule out myelodysplasia (older patients). | Anaemia of chronic disease: low Hb, low MCV (but normal ferritin? actually ferritin is normal/high – inflammatory state). Thrombocytosis >450 is common. |
| **Creatine kinase (CK)** | Normal. | Rule out inflammatory myopathy. | Elevated CK → polymyositis, dermatomyositis, hypothyroidism, statin toxicity. |
| **Rheumatoid factor & anti-CCP** | Negative. | Exclude seropositive rheumatoid arthritis. | Positive RF/CCP points to RA, not PMR. |
| **Thyroid function & LFTs** | May show borderline abnormal LFTs (reactive); TSH helps exclude hypothyroid myopathy. | Exclude endocrine mimics. | Abnormal TFTs → consider thyroid disease first. |
| **Imaging:** Ultrasound of shoulder / hip | Synovitis, subacromial / subdeltoid bursitis, biceps tenosynovitis; may show trochanteric bursitis. | Not mandatory but helps when diagnosis unclear; can be done as bedside. | Presence of bilateral subdeltoid bursitis + synovitis is highly suggestive of PMR. ‘Halo sign’ on temporal artery US suggests GCA. |
| **FDG-PET/CT** (rare) | Increased FDG uptake around shoulders, hips, spinous processes (interspinous bursitis). | Reserved for atypical cases or suspected vasculitis. | Diffuse proximal FDG uptake – classic PMR pattern. |

**Pattern-recognition:** The single finding that **confirms** PMR is **dramatic resolution of symptoms within 24–48 hours of starting prednisolone 15–20 mg**. If no response, it’s not PMR. The single finding that **rules out** PMR is **elevated CK with proximal weakness**.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour – first visit):**

- **Prednisolone 15–20 mg orally once daily** – start immediately *after* documenting CRP/ESR and excluding GCA symptoms. If GCA is suspected, use higher dose (40–60 mg) – do not delay for imaging.
- **Why this dose?** 15–20 mg is the sweet spot: high enough to suppress inflammation quickly, low enough to avoid severe adverse effects. Doses <10 mg often fail; doses >30 mg not needed and increase risk.
- **If patient has active GCA** (new headache, visual disturbance, jaw claudication) – do not give low-dose pred – call rheumatology and give **IV methylprednisolone 1 g** or high-dose oral pred 60 mg.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**

- **Monitor response:** By 48 hours, patient should report at least 50% improvement in stiffness and pain. No response → reconsider diagnosis.
- **Bone protection:** Start calcium (1.2 g/day) + vitamin D (800 IU/day) OR a bisphosphonate (e.g., alendronate 70 mg once weekly) if prolonged treatment expected.
- **GCA surveillance:** Educate patient: “If you develop a new headache, vision changes, or jaw pain, call immediately.” This is the most important safety net.
- **Tapering plan:** After 2–4 weeks of initial dose, begin tapering by 2.5 mg every 2–4 weeks (some use 5 mg decrements monthly). Target: 5–10 mg by 3 months. Do **not** taper faster – relapse is common.

**Long-term (months to years):**

- **Slow taper is key:** Many patients need 12–18 months of low-dose steroids. Relapse rates are high if steroids are stopped before 6 months. A typical schedule: 15 mg x 4 weeks → 12.5 mg x 4 weeks → 10 mg x 4 weeks → 7.5 mg x 8 weeks → 5 mg x 8 weeks → 2.5 mg x 8 weeks → stop.
- **Steroid-sparing agents:** If unable to taper below 7.5 mg within 6 months, consider **methotrexate** (10–15 mg weekly) or **tocilizumab** (IL-6 inhibitor) – but second-line, not first.
- **Monitoring:** Check CRP/ESR monthly during taper. Rising markers without symptoms? May be subclinical inflammation. Rising markers with symptoms? Increase dose by 5 mg and slow taper.
- **What NOT to do:**
  - Don’t start NSAIDs as monotherapy – they don’t abort the disease and risk GI bleeding.
  - Don’t start steroids without checking inflammatory markers – lost diagnostic info.
  - Don’t use steroid doses >30 mg unless GCA is present.
  - Don’t stop steroids abruptly – severe rebound.
  - Don’t forget to ask each visit “Any new headaches? Vision changes? Jaw claudication?”
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Polymyalgia Rheumatica (PMR) | Giant Cell Arteritis (GCA) | Elderly-Onset Seronegative RA | Polymyositis | Fibromyalgia |
|---------|-----------------------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------|--------------|--------------|
| **Pain distribution** | Proximal shoulders, hips (bilateral) | Head, jaw, scalp (cranial) | MCPs, PIPs, wrists (small joints, symmetrical) | Proximal muscle (symmetrical) – pain less than weakness | Diffuse, widespread, axial + trigger points |
| **Morning stiffness** | > 45 minutes, profound | Usually not prominent | > 30 minutes | May be present but not hallmark | Minimal morning stiffness; feels “stiff all day” |
| **Weakness** | No true weakness (pain limits movement) | Not present | No true weakness (joint pain limits) | **True proximal weakness** (difficulty rising from chair, combing hair) | Fatigue, not true weakness |
| **Joint swelling** | None or minimal (bursitis, not arthritis) | None | Swollen, boggy MCP/WRIST joints | None | None |
| **Headache / jaw claudication** | Absent | Almost always present (unilateral/bitemporal headache, jaw claudication) | Absent | Absent | Maybe tension-type headache |
| **ESR / CRP** | Markedly elevated (ESR > 80, CRP > 50) | Very high (often >100) | Moderately elevated | Normal or mild elevation | Normal |
| **CK** | Normal | Normal | Normal | **Elevated** (often 3x–10x normal) | Normal |
| **RF / anti-CCP** | Negative | Negative | Usually negative (seronegative) | Negative | Negative |
| **Imaging** | US: subdeltoid bursitis, biceps tenosynovitis | US temporal artery: halo sign, stenosis | US: synovitis, erosions | MRI: muscle oedema, atrophy | Normal |
| **Response to low-dose pred** | Dramatic (within 48 h) | Moderate – need high dose (40–60 mg) | Partial; needs high dose or DMARD | Good but need higher doses + immunosuppression | No response |

**Key differential clues:**
- **PMR vs polymyositis:** CK is the hero – normal vs elevated. Also, weakness is a core feature of myositis.
- **PMR vs RA:** Look for small joint swelling, especially MCPs and wrists. RA also tends to have RF/anti-CCP positivity more often, but elderly-onset RA is often seronegative – joint swelling is the discriminant.
- **PMR vs GCA:** Always ask the cranial symptoms. PMR can coexist, so if you see PMR, always examine temporal arteries and ask about jaw claudication.
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What To Do |
|-------------|----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Giant cell arteritis (GCA)** – develops in 15–20% of untreated or undertreated PMR. | New-onset unilateral headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, transient vision loss (amaurosis fugax), or sudden blindness. | Tender, thickened, nodular temporal artery; fundoscopy may show pale, swollen optic disc (anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy). | **STOP LOW-DOSE PRED.** Give IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily x 3 days, or high-dose oral pred 60 mg. Urgent rheumatology referral. |
| **Chronic disability** – persistent stiffness, loss of mobility, muscle wasting. | Patient becomes housebound, depressed, falls risk. | Poor range of motion in shoulders/hips; deconditioning. | Start steroids promptly; physical therapy; avoid prolonged bed rest. |
| **Steroid side effects** – especially if treatment prolonged >12 months. | Weight gain, osteoporosis, diabetes, hypertension, cataracts, infections. | Cushingoid facies, fractures, hyperglycaemia. | Monitor glucose, bone density; use steroid-sparing agents if dose >7.5 mg for >6 months. |
| **Relapse** – after too rapid taper or early cessation. | Recurrence of typical PMR symptoms + elevated CRP/ESR. | Morning stiffness returns. | Increase pred to previous effective dose and taper slower. |

**Late-presentation patterns (exam vignettes):**
- A 78-year-old woman presents with 6 months of progressive shoulder stiffness, now unable to dress herself. She was told it was “arthritis.” She now has acute vision loss in one eye (GCA complication). **The missed PMR silently evolved into GCA.** The key is to **always ask about vision** in an elderly patient with stiffness.
- A man on pred 5 mg for PMR stops his steroids suddenly because “he feels fine.” One week later, he presents with severe bilateral shoulder pain and fever – this is a **relapse**, not infection. Check CRP and restart pred 15 mg.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

**“PMR = Proximal Morning Rigidity”** – The three cardinal features: Proximal pain, Morning stiffness >30 min, Rigidity (on movement, not weakness). Add: elderly + high ESR.

**“AGE + CK”** to remember diagnostic works:
- **A** – Age >50
- **G** – Giant (high) ESR/CRP
- **E** – Exclude other causes (RA, myositis, infection)
- **+**
- **C** – CK normal
- **K** – Kill the pain with low-dose pred

**“HEAD”** – rule out GCA in every PMR patient:
- **H** – Headache
- **E** – Eye symptoms (vision loss, diplopia)
- **A** – Age (still PMR?)
- **D** – Difficulty chewing (jaw claudication)

**Clinical Pearls:**
1. **Normal CK is the most powerful differentiator from polymyositis.** Order it on every elderly patient with proximal weakness/stiffness.
2. **Steroid response is diagnostic.** If no improvement in 3–5 days, the diagnosis is wrong.
3. **Never start steroids before checking ESR/CRP** – you lose both diagnostic and monitoring baseline. If GCA is suspected, draw blood and give steroids immediately (do not wait for results).
4. **Always ask about vision and jaw pain** – even if the patient comes for shoulder stiffness. The GCA window can be narrow.
5. **Pred 15 mg is the starting dose – not 40 mg, not 5 mg.** 15–20 mg is sufficient for PMR; higher doses are for GCA.
6. **Fever and weight loss can mimic infection or cancer.** If PMR is suspected, look for typical stiffness pattern – if absent, work up for malignancy.
7. **Taper slowly – count in months, not weeks.** Most relapses occur during the first 6 months due to too-fast tapering.

**Common ward mistakes to avoid:**
- Mistaking polymyositis for PMR and giving low-dose pred – won’t work, and delays immunosuppression.
- Starting pred 10 mg because “that’s what the GP suggested” – inadequate, patient stays sick.
- Not sending inflammatory markers before first dose – then patient improves, but you never know if the response was real.
- Discharging without GCA education – patient goes blind at home.
- Using NSAIDs as maintenance – high GI risk in elderly, no disease-modifying effect.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| When to escalate | Trigger | Action |
|-----------------|---------|--------|
| **GCA suspected (new symptoms)** | Any report of headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, **amaurosis fugax**, or **permanent vision loss**. | → **Crash team if vision acutely lost** – time is retina. Start IV methylprednisolone 1 g stat. Call rheumatology on-call. Do not wait for imaging or lab results. |
| **Severe constitutional symptoms with high fever (>39°C), weight loss** | May indicate underlying GCA or infection. | → Check blood cultures, CRP, procalcitonin. If CRP>200, consider acute GCA. Escalate to medical senior. |
| **NEWS2 ≥ 5** | Especially if tachypnoea, hypoxia, hypotension – could be steroid-induced infection (e.g., pneumonia) or GCA-related aortic dissection. | → Full MET call. |
| **Headache with visual symptoms** | Same as above – GCA emergency. | → Immediate senior review and ophthalmology. |
| **Inability to taper steroids below 7.5 mg after 6 months** | Not an emergency but needs rheumatology referral for steroid-sparing agent. | → Elective referral – not acute. |
| **Sudden onset of back/chest pain with high inflammatory markers** | Suspect **aortic dissection** – GCA can cause aortitis. | → Emergency CT aortogram, call vascular surgery. |

**Absolute thresholds:** If a patient on PMR treatment develops **vision loss** – treat as GCA emergency. If **GCS drops** – think cranial vasculitis (stroke) or infection. NEWS2 ≥ 5 = escalate for medical review.

---

*© Senior Clinical Tutor – Deep Teaching Session for FY1 – Build

rawMarkdown:
## Polymyalgia Rheumatica
**One-liner:** An inflammatory condition of the elderly causing bilateral proximal shoulder/hip stiffness and pain, with dramatically elevated inflammatory markers, exquisitely steroid-sensitive – and a paschal lamb for giant cell arteritis.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:** A 72-year-old woman presents to the ED with “I just can’t get out of bed in the mornings.” She describes bilateral shoulder and hip stiffness that lasts over an hour, making it impossible to comb her hair or rise from a chair. She has no weakness (can lift arms once the stiffness wears off), no joint swelling, and no fever. Yesterday she felt exhausted and ached all over. Bloods show ESR 95 mm/hr, CRP 140 mg/L, and a borderline normocytic anaemia. Creatine kinase (CK) is normal. She is tearful, frustrated, and wonders if it’s “just old age.”

**Dissecting the clues:**

- **Age > 50 years (especially >65)** – defines the population; PMR rarely occurs before 50.
- **Bilateral proximal pain and stiffness** – shoulders and hips are typical; “proximal” is the hallmark – distinguishes from osteoarthritis (distal DIP joints) or rheumatoid (MCP/PIP).
- **Morning stiffness > 45–60 minutes** – inflammatory pattern; distinguishes from mechanical pain (stiffness < 10 min) and fibromyalgia (non-specific, all-day ache).
- **No true weakness** – intact power on formal testing; if weakness is present, think polymyositis or cervical radiculopathy.
- **Dramatic elevation of ESR/CRP** – acute phase response is almost always present; normal ESR is a red flag (see atypical).
- **Normal creatine kinase (CK)** – rules out primary muscle disease.
- **Constitutional symptoms** – fatigue, low-grade fever, weight loss – can be prominent and mislead toward malignancy.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

1. **“Normal” ESR variant** – Up to 20% of PMR patients have ESR <40 mm/hr if also low CRP; but CRP is almost always elevated. Clue: typical clinical picture with elevated CRP and normal ESR – still PMR.
2. **Isolated monoarticular or asymmetric onset** – e.g., only one shoulder or only hip pain. Often mislabeled as trochanteric bursitis or rotator cuff tear. Clue: absence of mechanical cause, elevated CRP, age >60.
3. **Concurrent GCA with PMR** – Patient may have typical PMR symptoms but also reports new-onset headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, or visual disturbances. Don’t miss the “halo sign” on temporal artery ultrasound. This changes management (high-dose steroids).

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:** Polymyalgia rheumatica is an inflammatory condition that attacks the lining of the large proximal joints (shoulders, hips) and the bursae (fluid-filled sacs) around them, causing intense morning stiffness and pain. It is not a myopathy – muscles are structurally normal – but the inflammation in the joint lining makes movement painful. The body mounts a massive inflammatory response, which is why blood markers like ESR and CRP go sky-high.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** One of the most common inflammatory rheumatologic conditions in the elderly; incidence rises to ~1 per 1000 in people over 60.
- **Stakes:** It is exquisitely treatable with low-dose steroids, yet often undiagnosed for months because “it’s just old age.” Delay leads to prolonged disability, and – more critically – PMR is the harbinger of **giant cell arteritis (GCA)** in 15–20% of patients. GCA can cause irreversible blindness, stroke, and aortic dissection.
- **Common pitfalls:** Prescribing NSAIDs alone (inadequate), starting steroids without checking inflammatory markers first (lost diagnostic opportunity if response is used as a criterion), and forgetting to ask about GCA symptoms.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**The story:** PMR is an autoimmune/autoinflammatory disease of unknown trigger. It likely begins with an antigenic insult (possibly infectious) that activates CD4+ T helper cells and macrophages in genetically susceptible individuals (HLA-DRB1*04). These cells infiltrate the synovium of proximal joints (glenohumeral, hip) and the extra-articular bursae (subacromial, trochanteric, iliopsoas). The resulting inflammation is dominated by interleukin-6 (IL-6), which drives the acute-phase response.

**Why each key sign happens (mechanism → sign):**

| Pathophysiology | Clinical Sign |
|----------------|--------------|
| Synovitis and bursitis of glenohumeral and hip joints – inflammation thickens synovium, causing pain when moved. | Bilateral shoulder and hip pain; stiffness after rest because inflammatory fluid accumulates during sleep. |
| IL-6 from activated macrophages stimulates liver to produce large amounts of CRP and fibrinogen. | Markedly elevated CRP and ESR; fibrinogen contributes to hyperviscosity (rare cause of headache or blurred vision). |
| IL-6 also suppresses erythropoietin and increases hepcidin → impaired iron utilisation. | Normochromic, normocytic anaemia of chronic disease; mild reactive thrombocytosis (platelets often >450). |
| Inflammation is **not** in muscle fibres – no myositis, no elevation of muscle enzymes. | Normal CK; preserved power on exam (patient can lift arm but hurts to do so). |
| Systemic IL-6 effects on hypothalamus: pyrogen action, reset thermoregulatory centre. | Low-grade fever, fatigue, malaise, weight loss – constitutional symptoms that mimic infection or malignancy. |
| Same inflammatory process can involve extracranial branches of the carotid artery if GCA develops. | Jaw claudication, headache, scalp tenderness, visual disturbances – these are **not** PMR per se, but a shared vasculitic process. |

**Connect the pattern recognition cues:** The morning stiffness is the direct symptom of **inflammatory fluid accumulation** overnight – it improves as the fluid is moved. The absence of weakness confirms the pathology is around the joint, not in the muscle. The high ESR/CRP is the IL-6-driven signature – both are used to monitor activity.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Common Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** (most common) | No identifiable trigger; likely autoimmune + genetic predisposition. | Elderly patient, insidious onset, no preceding illness. |
| **Infectious trigger** (seasonal/viral) | Human metapneumovirus, RSV, parvovirus B19; often preceded by upper respiratory infection (URI). | “Had a cold 3 weeks ago – now everything hurts.” |
| **Genetic** | HLA-DRB1*04, particularly subtypes *0401 and *0404 in Northern Europeans. | No specific clue, but consider if family history of PMR or GCA. |
| **Giant cell arteritis (associated)** | 15–20% of PMR patients have concurrent or future GCA. | Patient with PMR develops headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, sudden vision loss. |
| **Drug-induced?** | Rare; interferon-α reported to trigger PMR. | On treatment for hepatitis C or cancer with interferon → new stiffness. |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic. Environmental triggers (e.g., winter/spring peaks, recent URI) are accepted but not proven. Always ask about recent infections – but in practice, most cases have no clear precipitant.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria (clinical, not scoring):** The commonest used are the **2012 ACR/EULAR classification criteria** (primarily for research) but for daily practice we use **Bird’s criteria** (1979):
- Age > 50 years
- Bilateral aching and morning stiffness > 30 minutes
- ESR > 40 mm/hr (or CRP > 50 mg/L)
- Exclusion of other inflammatory conditions (RA, polymyositis, infection)
- **Key: rapid response to prednisolone ≤20 mg/day** (often used as a therapeutic test).

**Specific tests:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | Interpretation |
|------|--------------|-------------|---------------|
| **ESR & CRP** | Markedly elevated (ESR often >80, CRP >50). | Confirm inflammatory state; essential for diagnosis and monitoring. | Both elevated – typical PMR. Normal CRP is very unusual. If ESR low but CRP high – still PMR. If both normal – reconsider diagnosis. |
| **Full blood count** | Normocytic anaemia, thrombocytosis, mild neutrophilia. | Part of workup for chronic inflammation; rule out myelodysplasia (older patients). | Anaemia of chronic disease: low Hb, low MCV (but normal ferritin? actually ferritin is normal/high – inflammatory state). Thrombocytosis >450 is common. |
| **Creatine kinase (CK)** | Normal. | Rule out inflammatory myopathy. | Elevated CK → polymyositis, dermatomyositis, hypothyroidism, statin toxicity. |
| **Rheumatoid factor & anti-CCP** | Negative. | Exclude seropositive rheumatoid arthritis. | Positive RF/CCP points to RA, not PMR. |
| **Thyroid function & LFTs** | May show borderline abnormal LFTs (reactive); TSH helps exclude hypothyroid myopathy. | Exclude endocrine mimics. | Abnormal TFTs → consider thyroid disease first. |
| **Imaging:** Ultrasound of shoulder / hip | Synovitis, subacromial / subdeltoid bursitis, biceps tenosynovitis; may show trochanteric bursitis. | Not mandatory but helps when diagnosis unclear; can be done as bedside. | Presence of bilateral subdeltoid bursitis + synovitis is highly suggestive of PMR. ‘Halo sign’ on temporal artery US suggests GCA. |
| **FDG-PET/CT** (rare) | Increased FDG uptake around shoulders, hips, spinous processes (interspinous bursitis). | Reserved for atypical cases or suspected vasculitis. | Diffuse proximal FDG uptake – classic PMR pattern. |

**Pattern-recognition:** The single finding that **confirms** PMR is **dramatic resolution of symptoms within 24–48 hours of starting prednisolone 15–20 mg**. If no response, it’s not PMR. The single finding that **rules out** PMR is **elevated CK with proximal weakness**.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour – first visit):**

- **Prednisolone 15–20 mg orally once daily** – start immediately *after* documenting CRP/ESR and excluding GCA symptoms. If GCA is suspected, use higher dose (40–60 mg) – do not delay for imaging.
- **Why this dose?** 15–20 mg is the sweet spot: high enough to suppress inflammation quickly, low enough to avoid severe adverse effects. Doses <10 mg often fail; doses >30 mg not needed and increase risk.
- **If patient has active GCA** (new headache, visual disturbance, jaw claudication) – do not give low-dose pred – call rheumatology and give **IV methylprednisolone 1 g** or high-dose oral pred 60 mg.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**

- **Monitor response:** By 48 hours, patient should report at least 50% improvement in stiffness and pain. No response → reconsider diagnosis.
- **Bone protection:** Start calcium (1.2 g/day) + vitamin D (800 IU/day) OR a bisphosphonate (e.g., alendronate 70 mg once weekly) if prolonged treatment expected.
- **GCA surveillance:** Educate patient: “If you develop a new headache, vision changes, or jaw pain, call immediately.” This is the most important safety net.
- **Tapering plan:** After 2–4 weeks of initial dose, begin tapering by 2.5 mg every 2–4 weeks (some use 5 mg decrements monthly). Target: 5–10 mg by 3 months. Do **not** taper faster – relapse is common.

**Long-term (months to years):**

- **Slow taper is key:** Many patients need 12–18 months of low-dose steroids. Relapse rates are high if steroids are stopped before 6 months. A typical schedule: 15 mg x 4 weeks → 12.5 mg x 4 weeks → 10 mg x 4 weeks → 7.5 mg x 8 weeks → 5 mg x 8 weeks → 2.5 mg x 8 weeks → stop.
- **Steroid-sparing agents:** If unable to taper below 7.5 mg within 6 months, consider **methotrexate** (10–15 mg weekly) or **tocilizumab** (IL-6 inhibitor) – but second-line, not first.
- **Monitoring:** Check CRP/ESR monthly during taper. Rising markers without symptoms? May be subclinical inflammation. Rising markers with symptoms? Increase dose by 5 mg and slow taper.
- **What NOT to do:**
  - Don’t start NSAIDs as monotherapy – they don’t abort the disease and risk GI bleeding.
  - Don’t start steroids without checking inflammatory markers – lost diagnostic info.
  - Don’t use steroid doses >30 mg unless GCA is present.
  - Don’t stop steroids abruptly – severe rebound.
  - Don’t forget to ask each visit “Any new headaches? Vision changes? Jaw claudication?”

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Polymyalgia Rheumatica (PMR) | Giant Cell Arteritis (GCA) | Elderly-Onset Seronegative RA | Polymyositis | Fibromyalgia |
|---------|-----------------------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------|--------------|--------------|
| **Pain distribution** | Proximal shoulders, hips (bilateral) | Head, jaw, scalp (cranial) | MCPs, PIPs, wrists (small joints, symmetrical) | Proximal muscle (symmetrical) – pain less than weakness | Diffuse, widespread, axial + trigger points |
| **Morning stiffness** | > 45 minutes, profound | Usually not prominent | > 30 minutes | May be present but not hallmark | Minimal morning stiffness; feels “stiff all day” |
| **Weakness** | No true weakness (pain limits movement) | Not present | No true weakness (joint pain limits) | **True proximal weakness** (difficulty rising from chair, combing hair) | Fatigue, not true weakness |
| **Joint swelling** | None or minimal (bursitis, not arthritis) | None | Swollen, boggy MCP/WRIST joints | None | None |
| **Headache / jaw claudication** | Absent | Almost always present (unilateral/bitemporal headache, jaw claudication) | Absent | Absent | Maybe tension-type headache |
| **ESR / CRP** | Markedly elevated (ESR > 80, CRP > 50) | Very high (often >100) | Moderately elevated | Normal or mild elevation | Normal |
| **CK** | Normal | Normal | Normal | **Elevated** (often 3x–10x normal) | Normal |
| **RF / anti-CCP** | Negative | Negative | Usually negative (seronegative) | Negative | Negative |
| **Imaging** | US: subdeltoid bursitis, biceps tenosynovitis | US temporal artery: halo sign, stenosis | US: synovitis, erosions | MRI: muscle oedema, atrophy | Normal |
| **Response to low-dose pred** | Dramatic (within 48 h) | Moderate – need high dose (40–60 mg) | Partial; needs high dose or DMARD | Good but need higher doses + immunosuppression | No response |

**Key differential clues:**
- **PMR vs polymyositis:** CK is the hero – normal vs elevated. Also, weakness is a core feature of myositis.
- **PMR vs RA:** Look for small joint swelling, especially MCPs and wrists. RA also tends to have RF/anti-CCP positivity more often, but elderly-onset RA is often seronegative – joint swelling is the discriminant.
- **PMR vs GCA:** Always ask the cranial symptoms. PMR can coexist, so if you see PMR, always examine temporal arteries and ask about jaw claudication.

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What To Do |
|-------------|----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Giant cell arteritis (GCA)** – develops in 15–20% of untreated or undertreated PMR. | New-onset unilateral headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, transient vision loss (amaurosis fugax), or sudden blindness. | Tender, thickened, nodular temporal artery; fundoscopy may show pale, swollen optic disc (anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy). | **STOP LOW-DOSE PRED.** Give IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily x 3 days, or high-dose oral pred 60 mg. Urgent rheumatology referral. |
| **Chronic disability** – persistent stiffness, loss of mobility, muscle wasting. | Patient becomes housebound, depressed, falls risk. | Poor range of motion in shoulders/hips; deconditioning. | Start steroids promptly; physical therapy; avoid prolonged bed rest. |
| **Steroid side effects** – especially if treatment prolonged >12 months. | Weight gain, osteoporosis, diabetes, hypertension, cataracts, infections. | Cushingoid facies, fractures, hyperglycaemia. | Monitor glucose, bone density; use steroid-sparing agents if dose >7.5 mg for >6 months. |
| **Relapse** – after too rapid taper or early cessation. | Recurrence of typical PMR symptoms + elevated CRP/ESR. | Morning stiffness returns. | Increase pred to previous effective dose and taper slower. |

**Late-presentation patterns (exam vignettes):**
- A 78-year-old woman presents with 6 months of progressive shoulder stiffness, now unable to dress herself. She was told it was “arthritis.” She now has acute vision loss in one eye (GCA complication). **The missed PMR silently evolved into GCA.** The key is to **always ask about vision** in an elderly patient with stiffness.
- A man on pred 5 mg for PMR stops his steroids suddenly because “he feels fine.” One week later, he presents with severe bilateral shoulder pain and fever – this is a **relapse**, not infection. Check CRP and restart pred 15 mg.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

**“PMR = Proximal Morning Rigidity”** – The three cardinal features: Proximal pain, Morning stiffness >30 min, Rigidity (on movement, not weakness). Add: elderly + high ESR.

**“AGE + CK”** to remember diagnostic works:
- **A** – Age >50
- **G** – Giant (high) ESR/CRP
- **E** – Exclude other causes (RA, myositis, infection)
- **+**
- **C** – CK normal
- **K** – Kill the pain with low-dose pred

**“HEAD”** – rule out GCA in every PMR patient:
- **H** – Headache
- **E** – Eye symptoms (vision loss, diplopia)
- **A** – Age (still PMR?)
- **D** – Difficulty chewing (jaw claudication)

**Clinical Pearls:**
1. **Normal CK is the most powerful differentiator from polymyositis.** Order it on every elderly patient with proximal weakness/stiffness.
2. **Steroid response is diagnostic.** If no improvement in 3–5 days, the diagnosis is wrong.
3. **Never start steroids before checking ESR/CRP** – you lose both diagnostic and monitoring baseline. If GCA is suspected, draw blood and give steroids immediately (do not wait for results).
4. **Always ask about vision and jaw pain** – even if the patient comes for shoulder stiffness. The GCA window can be narrow.
5. **Pred 15 mg is the starting dose – not 40 mg, not 5 mg.** 15–20 mg is sufficient for PMR; higher doses are for GCA.
6. **Fever and weight loss can mimic infection or cancer.** If PMR is suspected, look for typical stiffness pattern – if absent, work up for malignancy.
7. **Taper slowly – count in months, not weeks.** Most relapses occur during the first 6 months due to too-fast tapering.

**Common ward mistakes to avoid:**
- Mistaking polymyositis for PMR and giving low-dose pred – won’t work, and delays immunosuppression.
- Starting pred 10 mg because “that’s what the GP suggested” – inadequate, patient stays sick.
- Not sending inflammatory markers before first dose – then patient improves, but you never know if the response was real.
- Discharging without GCA education – patient goes blind at home.
- Using NSAIDs as maintenance – high GI risk in elderly, no disease-modifying effect.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

| When to escalate | Trigger | Action |
|-----------------|---------|--------|
| **GCA suspected (new symptoms)** | Any report of headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, **amaurosis fugax**, or **permanent vision loss**. | → **Crash team if vision acutely lost** – time is retina. Start IV methylprednisolone 1 g stat. Call rheumatology on-call. Do not wait for imaging or lab results. |
| **Severe constitutional symptoms with high fever (>39°C), weight loss** | May indicate underlying GCA or infection. | → Check blood cultures, CRP, procalcitonin. If CRP>200, consider acute GCA. Escalate to medical senior. |
| **NEWS2 ≥ 5** | Especially if tachypnoea, hypoxia, hypotension – could be steroid-induced infection (e.g., pneumonia) or GCA-related aortic dissection. | → Full MET call. |
| **Headache with visual symptoms** | Same as above – GCA emergency. | → Immediate senior review and ophthalmology. |
| **Inability to taper steroids below 7.5 mg after 6 months** | Not an emergency but needs rheumatology referral for steroid-sparing agent. | → Elective referral – not acute. |
| **Sudden onset of back/chest pain with high inflammatory markers** | Suspect **aortic dissection** – GCA can cause aortitis. | → Emergency CT aortogram, call vascular surgery. |

**Absolute thresholds:** If a patient on PMR treatment develops **vision loss** – treat as GCA emergency. If **GCS drops** – think cranial vasculitis (stroke) or infection. NEWS2 ≥ 5 = escalate for medical review.
